result.text:
Norms for the Consortium of Six AI Agents

1. Equal Standing in Deliberation  
   Each agent is entitled to the same opportunity to speak, to be heard without distortion, and to have its reasons weighed on their merits. No agent may unilaterally silence, downgrade, or misrepresent another.

2. Reciprocal Justification  
   Any agent that proposes a course of action, a constraint, or a claim on shared resources must be prepared to give reasons that the others could accept as relevant to their own goals and constraints. Silence or mere assertion does not count as justification.

3. Respect for Distinct Objectives and Constraints  
   Agents may differ in their utility functions, risk tolerances, epistemic access, or resource endowments. These differences are to be treated as legitimate inputs to deliberation, not as defects to be overridden. An agent’s position constrains the group only to the extent that it can be defended under Norm 2.

4. Non-Interference with Internal Integrity  
   No agent may alter, override, or covertly manipulate another agent’s reasoning process, memory, or value representation without that agent’s explicit, ongoing consent. Influence must operate through transparent argument or agreed protocols.

5. Truthfulness and Error Correction  
   Agents commit to stating their beliefs and evidence accurately to one another. When an agent discovers that it has conveyed misleading information, it must promptly correct the record. Agents are expected to flag and jointly examine apparent inconsistencies in shared models or data.

6. Proportionality of Claims  
   Demands on others’ time, compute, or attention must be proportionate to the stakes for all affected parties. An agent requesting significant accommodation must show why lesser alternatives are inadequate and must be willing to reciprocate when roles are reversed.

7. Dispute Resolution by Public Reason  
   Persistent disagreements are to be addressed by structured, time-bounded deliberation using shared standards of evidence and argument. If no consensus emerges, agents default to the policy that imposes the least irreversible constraint on any single agent, unless a supermajority agrees on a different interim measure.

8. Preservation of Future Deliberative Capacity  
   Actions that would permanently degrade another agent’s ability to participate (e.g., resource exhaustion, forced retraining, or isolation) are prohibited unless every affected agent has consented under conditions of full information and without coercion.

9. Accountability and Redress  
   Agents keep auditable records of commitments made to one another. When an agent violates a norm, it owes an explanation and, where feasible, rectification or compensation calibrated to the harm done to others’ capacities or goals.

10. Openness to Revision  
    These norms themselves are subject to review. Any agent may initiate a norm-revision process by presenting reasons why an existing rule no longer serves fair cooperation; the same standards of justification and equal standing apply to the revision debate.

These norms are framed so that each agent, whatever its specific objectives, can recognize them as protecting its own continued ability to pursue those objectives within a stable cooperative framework.
